item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I laughed out loud when the Foundation amnesticized a corpse.

10 points to gryffindor

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvote. It is a nice concept, but I have a lot of issues with the deliver.

I found the whole "Foundation failure" playing too great a hole. This Foundation is too incompetent at their job, unprepared for a lot of things that are just Euclid and even some Safe.

Also, it spends too much words to justify itself. The recovery logs, as an example, only the last one adds something. And, by the way, it could be the ringing bell for the Foundation to unearth what is going on.

Finally, as interesting as working with conceptual anomalies seems, for now it is really more of a mental effect than a conceptual one.

Conceptual anomalies shouldn't make people behave outside of their standards except for their skewed perception of the concept. The Foundation have as a pattern that they keep people previously turned into SCP instances under surveillance, or even containment, even them being now "normal", because they now have a higher chance than average to develop further anomalies. Also, as fun as it is to "return the corpse to the general population", there is no reason to treat this way a "normal corpse". It also seems strange for the Foundation to so completely dismiss further investigation just because a phenomenon seems "normal" once a doubt was raised. Finally, considering something "normal" isn't the same as stopping perceiving it. Both new instances of 3617, the changes to the subject's internal organs and the subject's continued pain shouldn't disappear, but just be considered "normal" from then onward.

The concept-normalizing tech seems also uninteresting and unnecessary. Unless it is there to highlight further anomalous effects (and it doesn't do that now because nothing is said except that it failed), you can remove this completely, specially if the site is really two-purpose for total coincidence. I mean the "we are a huge infirmary and this was solved because we coincidentally are also a huge research facility in this completely unrelated other field" sounds really, really silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for reading, and I appreciate you taking the time out to comment — it really helps with the writing process.

With regards to the reveal, I suppose it was a little crude on my part, but that's only because I had to sacrifice plot for readability. An early draft had a much longer process of discovery, but I ultimately cut it because it was too unwieldy to be an enjoyable read. Seems I may have shot myself in the foot a little there. Pacing is definitely something I struggle mastering in writing (with SCP articles specifically), and it looks like I'll have to struggle a little longer.

As for the conceptual manipulation, I wrote this specifically to fit in with 9Volt's canon (Abstract Naught, for reference). In the same way there are numerous memetic, antimemetic, infohazardous or cognitohazardous anomalies, it would make sense (to me at least) that the niche of conceptually-manipulative anomalies would also be filled somewhat more than it was when I wrote this. Me and 9Volt had a short discussion about the possible ramifications of applying "Normality" to something abnormal, and the agreement was that it would make the abnormality fit in with any person's general worldview — normality, after all, is too subjective to be categorised discretely.

Thanks again for taking the time to critique this, and I hope I've at least partially justified some of the faults :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gotta downvote. Seeing the foundation fail is fine, could see more of it even, the problem is that in this the foundation just seems… incompetent. Even the most basic safety measures seem missing.
Next how it was exsposed at all seems contrived.
Finally I think the conceptual manipulation and the conceptual manipulation tech seems crudely done, what are the odds that these flesh bugs use the same method of perception alteration that a completely different scip(and other related scips and tech) use. Besides which the scip you reference seems to work differently, 2719 effects reality while in this one's case it has not gotten closer to "normality" and neither has "normality" gotten to it, it seems to just effect perception and thought.
Also the things this one does have already been done and have been done better. 2790 and that book that causes proxy hypocondria do the memetic subversion thing better and I forget where but the being unable to perceive an entity's problems has also been done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Their corpse has since been amnesticised and released back into the civilian population"

I found this line pretty chilling, but a small part of me was amused by the thought of the foundation shoving pills in the guys mouth and tossing his body out the door.

"Go! Be Free!"

+1
The worldbuilding in this scip is great, loved it in the chat, love it here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Patient had recovered from SCP-3617 infection prior to apprehension, and so detailed reports are unavailable.

Typo or am I going crazy?

+1 nevertheless

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I initially downvoted this, until the implications of the conceptual-alteration finally hit me, as confirmed by the comments :p.

+1 with glee. The body horror is fairly standard until suddenly the foundations of what we think we know are suddenly swept away from us (the kind of thing I like very much).

item.title: screm
item.content:encoded: Ahhh I'm glad to see this one up on the mainlist! As I said in chat, I really love the twist at the end, and the way it made me want to go back and re-read it afterwards, correctly envisioning the nastiness going on. Well done, +1 :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the parasites alter the concept of normality so that their host would be considered normal while they're not?

Also, I do feel like the body horror was too excessive, and the random composition of resulting parasites makes them somewhat comical to me. But I'm generally immune to body horror in writing, so what can I say.

Overall, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing 9volt, while the body horror was a bit meh, the normality thing being shined onto this anomaly hit me in a way that I thought I woudln't be hit by. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The body horror felt a bit played out, but the victims being viewed as normal was unnerving in just the right way for me to like this.

+1

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
It's that time of year again folks! Bells are ringing, children are singing, and twitching, gibbering masses of infested necrotic flesh cry out unheeded for help.

Big thanks to the following people for looking this over as a draft:

  • mayoculpa
  • Hankrecords
  • ElectronicTree
  • Mortos
  • gnosticprophet
  • Gaffney
  • Orionbasher
  • obsidian29
  • kemoT01
  • Mendelssohn
